title: Isaiah 8
subtitle: Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz

heb.gloss: quick to the plunder, swift to the spoil
heb.paragraph: "Name him Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz" (v.3). Isaiah’s second son: the longest personal name in the Bible, meaning "quick to plunder, swift to spoil." Before the child can say "mother" or "father," Damascus and Samaria will be plundered (v.4). Because Judah rejected the gentle waters of Shiloah, the Euphrates flood (Assyria) will sweep through, reaching even Judah "up to the neck" (vv.7–8). But: "God is with us" (ʿimmānū ʾēl, v.10).

refs.note: Jesus sends the blind man to wash in the Pool of Siloam — the same Shiloah waters, the gentle provision Judah once rejected.
Immanuel — the name given in ch.7 returns as a confession in 8:8, 10.
Matthew quotes Isa 9:1–2 (which follows from this passage) for Jesus’ Galilean ministry.
A stone of stumbling — Paul quotes Isa 8:14.

notes.note: Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz: the child’s name IS the prophecy. Before he can speak, the enemy falls.
Gentle Shiloah rejected; Euphrates flood comes. The flood reaches Judah’s neck. Drowning is imminent but not complete.
"Devise your strategy, but it will be thwarted. For God is with us." The Immanuel confession as defiance.
Calvin reads the water-contrast as a universal principle: when people reject God’s gentle provision, they receive overwhelming alternatives. The quiet stream rejected becomes a roaring river imposed.
Calvin notes the Immanuel confession (v.10) comes AFTER the flood announcement: faith declares God’s presence not in the absence of crisis but in its midst.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET identifies the "waters of Shiloah" with the Gihon Spring, Jerusalem’s primary water source — modest compared to the Euphrates but life-giving for the city.
The NET notes that "stone of stumbling and rock of offence" is quoted by both Paul (Rom 9:33) and Peter (1 Pet 2:8) for Christ. Jesus is Immanuel: salvation for believers, stumbling for rejecters.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads the two-child naming (Shear-Jashub in ch.7, Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz in ch.8) as Isaiah’s prophetic-family ministry: his household IS his message. Names, children, encounters are all prophetic signs.

Oswalt: the Shiloah/Euphrates contrast is the book’s clearest expression of the faith-vs-politics choice: trust the quiet provision of God or the overwhelming force of empire. There is no middle ground.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the passage as the escalation of the Immanuel theology: the name spoken as sign (7:14), as address (8:8), and as confession (8:10). Each usage deepens the meaning.
Childs notes the "sanctuary or stumbling stone" duality: God’s presence is either refuge or ruin, depending on the receiver’s response. The same God, opposite outcomes.

hist.context: The "waters of Shiloah" (v.6) is the gentle spring that supplied Jerusalem — a metaphor for God’s quiet provision. Judah rejected the gentle for the dramatic: Assyria’s Euphrates-like military flood. The Immanuel name returns (v.8, 10) as both address to the land and defiant confession: despite the flood, God IS with us. Isaiah’s family becomes a "sign and symbol" for Israel (v.18). The two children’s names (Shear-Jashub, Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz) together prophesy: swift judgment, but a remnant returns.

sections.header: Verses 11–22 — “To the Law and to the Testimony”

heb.paragraph: "To the law (tōrâ) and to the testimony! If they do not speak according to this word, they have no light of dawn" (v.20). God warns Isaiah not to follow the people’s fear (v.11). The LORD Almighty is the one you are to dread (v.13). He will be a sanctuary — or a stone of stumbling (v.14). Isaiah will bind up the testimony among his disciples (v.16). "I will wait for the LORD, who is hiding his face from Jacob" (v.17). Isaiah and his children are signs (v.18). When they say "consult the dead," respond: to the LAW and the TESTIMONY (vv.19–20).

refs.note: The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone; a stone of stumbling — Peter quotes Isa 8:14.
I lay in Zion a stone that causes people to stumble — Paul combines Isa 8:14 with 28:16.
Let no one be found among you who practises divination or consults the dead — the Torah basis for Isa 8:19’s prohibition.
Simeon: This child is destined to cause the falling and rising of many in Israel — echoing Isa 8:14’s stumbling stone.

notes.note: Fear God, not circumstances. He will be either sanctuary OR stumbling stone. Same God, opposite outcomes depending on response.
"Bind up the testimony among my disciples." Isaiah creates a community of faithfulness. "I and the children the LORD has given me are signs and symbols in Israel."
Consult the dead? NO. "To the law and to the testimony!" The written word as the standard against which all claims are tested.
Darkness, distress, gloom. The chapter ends at the lowest point — setting up the magnificent contrast of 9:1–2.

Calvin reads the sanctuary-or-stumbling duality as the fundamental human choice: the same Christ is salvation for those who trust and ruin for those who reject. The object does not change; the response determines the outcome.

Calvin makes this verse programmatic: all spiritual claims must be tested against written Scripture. No experience, no vision, no voice overrides the word. This becomes a Reformation principle: sola Scriptura is anticipated by Isaiah.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "disciples" (limmudim) is the same word used in 50:4 (the Servant’s "instructed tongue"). Isaiah’s disciples and the Servant’s disciples share vocabulary.
The NET observes the phrase "no light of dawn" (shachar) puns on the darkness of v.22. Without Torah, there is no morning. The word IS the light.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads the passage as Isaiah’s personal commission within the larger mission: he cannot change the nation, but he can preserve the testimony among a faithful few. The remnant theology becomes practical.
Oswalt: "to the law and to the testimony" is Isaiah’s hermeneutical principle. When confusion reigns, return to Scripture. The principle is as relevant today as in the 8th century BC.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the "binding of the testimony" as the canonical moment: Isaiah’s oracles are entrusted to a community of transmission. This is the origin of the book of Isaiah as a written document — preserved by disciples across generations.
Childs notes the darkness-ending deliberately precedes 9:1–2’s great light. The canonical arrangement is not accidental: maximum darkness before maximum light.

hist.context: The "bind up the testimony, seal the law among my disciples" (v.16) is often read as the moment Isaiah creates a circle of followers who preserve his teaching — possibly the origin of the "disciples of Isaiah" who may have compiled Second and Third Isaiah. The rejection of necromancy (v.19) addresses the desperate search for guidance when God seems silent. Isaiah’s answer: the written word (Torah and testimony) is the standard, not spiritual experimentation. The passage ends in darkness and distress (vv.21–22) — which sets up the great light of 9:1–2.

ppl.text: Isaiah’s family becomes a "sign and symbol" for Israel (v…

ppl.name: remnant
ppl.text: The two children’s names (Shear-Jashub, Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz) together prophesy: swift judgment, but a remnant returns…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe Lord said to me, \u201cTake a large scroll and write on it with an ordinary pen: Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz.\u201dESVThen the LORD said to me, \u201cTake a large tablet and write on it in common characters, \u2018Belonging to Maher-shalal-hash-baz.\u2019NIVSo I called in Uriah the priest and Zechariah son of Jeberekiah as reliable witnesses for me.ESVAnd I will get reliable witnesses, Uriah the priest and Zechariah the son of Jeberechiah, to attest for me.\u201d

src.title: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC)
src.quote: The Cyrus Cylinder relates to Deutero-Isaiah (chapters 40-55), not Isaiah chapter 1 being discussed here.
src.note: The cylinder confirms Isaiah’s prophecy of a Persian king who would liberate captives and authorize temple rebuilding, whether the prophecy is dated early (8th c.) or late (6th c.).

src.title: The Sennacherib Prism (c.700 BC)
src.quote: The Sennacherib Prism relates to events described in Isaiah 36-39, not Isaiah chapter 1.
src.note: The silence confirms Isaiah 36–37: Sennacherib besieged but never conquered Jerusalem. His own records inadvertently verify Isaiah’s narrative of divine deliverance.

src.title: The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A complete copy of Isaiah found at Qumran, dated c.125 BC. The oldest complete biblical manuscript, it is virtually identical to the Masoretic text 1,000 years later.
src.note: The scroll demonstrates the remarkable textual stability of Isaiah across a millennium. The text the church reads today is essentially the text the Qumran community read.

rec.text: "Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz: the child’s name IS the prophecy. Before he can speak, the enemy falls."

rows.label: Law and Testimony — 8:11–22
rows.text: Sanctuary OR stumbling stone. "To the LAW and the TESTIMONY." Darkness — setting up the great light.

hebtext.gloss: quick to the plunder, swift to the spoil
hebtext.note: "Name him Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz" (v.3). Isaiah’s second son: the longest personal name in the Bible, meaning "quick to plunder, swift to spoil." Before the child can say "mother" or "father," Damascus and Samaria will be plundered (v.4). Because Judah rejected the gentle waters of Shiloah, the Euphrates flood (Assyria) will sweep through, reaching even Judah "up to the neck" (vv.7–8). But: "God is with us" (ʿimmānū ʾēl, v.10).

"To the law (tōrâ) and to the testimony! If they do not speak according to this word, they have no light of dawn" (v.20). God warns Isaiah not to follow the people’s fear (v.11). The LORD Almighty is the one you are to dread (v.13). He will be a sanctuary — or a stone of stumbling (v.14). Isaiah will bind up the testimony among his disciples (v.16). "I will wait for the LORD, who is hiding his face from Jacob" (v.17). Isaiah and his children are signs (v.18). When they say "consult the dead," respond: to the LAW and the TESTIMONY (vv.19–20).

thread.text: Jesus sends the blind man to wash in the Pool of Siloam — the same Shiloah waters, the gentle provision Judah once rejected.
Immanuel — the name given in ch.7 returns as a confession in 8:8, 10.
Matthew quotes Isa 9:1–2 (which follows from this passage) for Jesus’ Galilean ministry.
A stone of stumbling — Paul quotes Isa 8:14.
The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone; a stone of stumbling — Peter quotes Isa 8:14.
I lay in Zion a stone that causes people to stumble — Paul combines Isa 8:14 with 28:16.

themes.note: Faith peaks: Immanuel as defiant confession amid the flood. Prophecy: stumbling stone (Rom 9:33; 1 Pet 2:8). Law and testimony as the standard.

coaching.tip: Isaiah names his son Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz — 'quick to the plunder, swift to the spoil.' This child is a walking prophecy, a living billboard. Isaiah uses his own family as prophetic signs (his other son Shear-Jashub means 'a remnant will return'). The prophet's personal life becomes the message. Embodied prophecy costs something the spoken word doesn't.

'To the law and to the testimony!' (v. 20) becomes Isaiah's test for true versus false guidance. When people suggest consulting mediums and spiritists, the answer is Scripture. This verse became a Reformation watchword — sola scriptura in seed form. The principle is simple: any revelation that contradicts prior revelation is self-disqualifying.

prayer_prompt: As Isaiah binds up his testimony and seals it among his disciples while the nation chases after mediums and spiritists, consider asking God what voices compete with His word for your attention.
